title: Past Tense & Expressing Desire
learningGoal: The learner will conjugate verbs and adjectives into the past and past-negative tenses, and use the suffix '~tai desu' to express desire.
instructions: This lesson unlocks your ability to talk about what you DID yesterday, and what you WANT TO DO tomorrow. Pay special attention to how i-adjectives conjugate in the past tense.

lessonContent.bodyText: Conjugating polite verbs into the past tense is incredibly easy. Just change 'masu' to 'mashita'. To make it negative past (didn't do), change 'masen' to 'masen deshita'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: たべます -> たべました
たべません -> たべませんでした
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I do not eat -> I did not eat

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: きのう、えいがを みました。

lessonContent.bodyText: To say something WAS an i-adjective (e.g. 'It was expensive'), drop the final 'i' and add 'katta'. For negative past ('It was not expensive'), drop 'i' and add 'kunakatta'.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: たかい -> たかかった です
たかくない -> たかくなかった です
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Not expensive -> It was not expensive

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: テストは むずかしかった です。

lessonContent.bodyText: For na-adjectives, the adjective itself does not change. Instead, 'desu' changes to 'deshita' (was). Negative becomes 'ja arimasen deshita' (was not).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: しずか です -> しずか でした
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: It is quiet -> It was quiet

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: しずかじゃありません -> しずかじゃありません でした
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Shizuka ja arimasen -> Shizuka ja arimasen deshita
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: It is not quiet -> It was not quiet

lessonContent.bodyText: To say 'I want to (do something)', take a verb, drop the 'masu', and add 'tai desu'. Once you add 'tai', the verb acts EXACTLY like an i-adjective!

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: たべます -> たべたい です
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I eat -> I want to eat

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: いきます -> いきたい です
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I go -> I want to go

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: のみたくない です。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I don't want to drink (Nomitai drops 'i' + kunai).

learningObjective: The learner will master past tense conjugations across all noun/adjective/verb classes, and synthesize the '~tai' form to express desires.

examples.targetText: きのう、すしを たべました。とても おいしかった です。
examples.transliteration: Kinou, sushi wo tabemashita. Totemo oishikatta desu.
examples.baseTranslation: Yesterday, I ate sushi. It was very delicious.
examples.explanation: Combines a past-tense verb (tabemashita) and a past-tense i-adjective (oishikatta).

examples.targetText: きょうは あつい です。みずを のみたい です。
examples.transliteration: Kyou wa atsui desu. Mizu wo nomitai desu.
examples.baseTranslation: Today is hot. I want to drink water.
examples.explanation: Uses present tense adjective (atsui) and the desire form of drink (nomimasu -> nomitai).

importantRules.ruleText: Once a verb becomes '~tai', it behaves entirely like an i-adjective.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Want to eat: Tabetai. Don't want to eat: Tabetakunai. Wanted to eat: Tabetakatta. Didn't want to eat: Tabetakunakatta.

importantRules.ruleText: You cannot use '~tai desu' to say what SOMEONE ELSE wants to do. You can only use it for your own desires, or to ask a direct question ('Do you want to go?').
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Saying 'Tanaka-san wa ikitai desu' is unnatural. Japanese requires different grammar to guess someone else's feelings.

cultureContext.contextText: When refusing an invitation, Japanese people rarely say 'I don't want to go' (Ikitakunai desu) because it is too direct and harsh. Instead, they say 'Chotto...' (It's a little...) and trail off, letting the other person understand.
dosList: Use ~tai desu to express your own hopes enthusiastically (e.g. 'I want to go to Japan!').
dontsList: Do not use ~takunai desu to refuse an invitation from a friend. Use a softer excuse.

summary: Verbs in the past end in ~mashita. i-Adjectives in the past end in ~katta. na-Adjectives in the past end in ~deshita. To say 'I want to...', drop ~masu and add ~tai desu.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What is the past tense of 'Samui' (Cold)?
How do you say 'I didn't go'?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: いきませんでした (Ikimasen deshita)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: How do you say 'I want to see' (from Mimasu)?

adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Introduction to temporal shifts and expression of desire.
importantFacts: ~tai turns a verb into an i-adjective grammatically.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must confidently manipulate past tense markers across verbs and adjectives.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You now have the power to talk about memories and dreams.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the i-adjective past tense. It can be a tongue-twister. Takai -> Takakatta.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Remember that ~tai desu only applies to YOUR desires, not your friend's.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: たべます。たべました。たべません。たべませんでした。きのう、えいがを みました。たかい。たかかった です。たかくない。たかくなかった です。テストは むずかしかった です。しずか でした。しずかじゃありません でした。たべたい です。いきたい です。のみたくない です。
